Your Colorado 529 CollegeInvest contribution of $300 per month is a thoughtful start, and looking at your full financial picture helps put it in proper context.
Since this involves education savings strategy and tax considerations, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or financial planner for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
What your data shows
From your May transactions, here's a snapshot of your goal-directed saving each month:
Retirement — Salesforce 401(k): $700 per semi-monthly paycheck × 2 = $1,400/month employee deferral (8% of $8,750 gross)
Retirement — UCHealth 401(k): $300 per semi-monthly paycheck × 2 = $600/month employee deferral (6% of $5,000 gross)
HSA: $400/month contribution to HealthEquity
529 — Colorado CollegeInvest: $300/month
So in total, your intentional goal-directed contributions come to $2,700/month — a genuinely strong savings discipline given your household's dual-income structure.
How the 529 stacks up
Your combined gross income works out to approximately $166,500 annually ($8,750 + $5,000 semi-monthly gross × 24 payments), and your take-home (net) in May was $16,650 from paychecks. The $300/month to the 529 represents roughly 1.8% of your gross income — modest relative to the retirement effort, but not unreasonable for a household also managing a $3,850 mortgage, $1,650 in daycare, and a $485 auto loan.
The key question is what college funding target you're working toward. As a rough benchmark, $300/month started early (say, at birth) growing at a historical average of around 6–7% annually could potentially accumulate to approximately $100,000–$120,000 over 18 years — a meaningful contribution toward Colorado in-state tuition costs, but likely a partial funding strategy for a private institution or out-of-state school.
A few considerations worth weighing
Colorado state tax deduction: Contributions to CollegeInvest are deductible on your Colorado state return with no annual cap, so every dollar you contribute reduces your state taxable income — a meaningful benefit given Colorado's 4.40% flat rate.
Retirement first is generally sound: Your 401(k) contributions are appropriately prioritized. Retirement accounts have tax advantages, no equivalent financial aid impact, and can't be funded retroactively — children have other paths (loans, scholarships, work) that retirees don't.
Daycare as a temporary expense: Your $1,650–$1,870/month in childcare (Bright Horizons + Care.com backup) will eventually roll off. When that happens, redirecting even a portion toward the 529 could meaningfully accelerate college savings without changing your lifestyle.
HSA as a triple tax-advantaged asset: Your $400/month HSA is excellent — it's one of the most tax-efficient vehicles available and doubles as a healthcare safety net and long-term investment account.
Overall, your $300/month 529 contribution is reasonable given your current fixed obligations, but has natural room to grow as childcare costs wind down. The retirement and HSA prioritization is exactly the right sequencing.
Would you like to explore what your 529 balance might look like at different contribution levels, or how the Colorado state deduction factors into the overall value of the account?
